paper.title_en: The SWEET Project: Probing Sugar Crystals for Direct Dark Matter Searches
paper.abstract_en: Several experiments are currently competing to directly detect sub-GeV/c<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$^{2}$</tex-math></inline-formula> dark matter through elastic scattering off various target nuclei. Hydrogen-rich materials, such as organic compounds, are promising candidate targets for such experiments due to favorable kinematics. In this article, we present for the first time results obtained with a sugar-based cryogenic particle detector. A sucrose (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf {C_{12}H_{22}O_{11}}$</tex-math></inline-formula>) monocrystal was instrumented with a neutron transmutation-doped germanium thermistor to detect phonons from particle interactions and was operated in the vicinity of a light detector. Particle interactions in the sugar were observed with associated scintillation light, indicating a possible channel for particle discrimination.
